id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0000
text: ![](https://assets.abnamro.com/api/public/content/shutterstock-1654094053-landscape-4x1.jpg?t=h440)

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0000
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0001
text: [Analysis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/analyses/index.html)

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0001
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0002
text: [Technology, Media & Telecom](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/technologie/index.html)

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0002
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0003
text: [Julia Krauwer](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/auteurs/julia-krauwer/index.html)

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0003
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0004
text: After years of lagging behind tech giants from the United States and China, the European Union is focusing on “digital sovereignty”. This means being less dependent on foreign technology by investing in a thriving domestic tech ecosystem. The road is still long, but there are hopeful signs.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0004
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0005
text: At the end of March, thirteen European ministers met to discuss digitalisation and the tech sector. A key topic was Europe’s digital autonomy — or rather its lack, which has been seen as a greater vulnerability since President Trump took office. Calls for digital self-sufficiency are growing louder on four fronts in particular: the cloud, artificial intelligence, the business climate for young technology companies, and the chip industry.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0005
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0006
text: During the summit, the ministers decided, among other things, to fast-track the revision of the European Chips Act — previously planned for 2026 — to accelerate investment in the semiconductor sector and reduce dependence on Asian suppliers. According to the ministers, strategic technologies such as chips, artificial intelligence (AI) and the cloud must also be addressed in conjunction. They also advocate larger public procurement to help local players scale up.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0006
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0007
text: A majority of European governments and companies run on US-origin cloud infrastructure: Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud. Together these parties control around 70 to 80 per cent of the European [cloud market](https://sciencebusiness.net/news/ict/new-franco-german-cloud-computing-project-set-standards-and-challenge-us-market-leaders#:~:text=However%2C%20with%20almost%2070%20per,for%20Cyber%20Studies%20%26%20Research). The remainder is fragmented across dozens of smaller providers, including European players such as SAP, Deutsche Telekom, OVHcloud and Orange. No European player commands more than a few per cent market share. SAP and Deutsche Telekom top the European ranking with just [2 per cent of the market](https://www.srgresearch.com/articles/european-cloud-providers-continue-to-grow-but-still-lose-market-share#:~:text=been%20Amazon%2C%20Microsoft%20and%20Google,list%20of%20national%20and%20regional) each.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0007
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0008
text: The Dutch government also appears to be heavily dependent on cloud services from the US tech giants. Almost half (44 per cent) of the cloud services the government uses fall into the “public cloud” category and therefore rely on the infrastructure of these parties. In many cases, a risk assessment has not been carried out, according to a recent report by the Algemene Rekenkamer with the ominous subtitle “Dark clouds are gathering”. According to Simon Besteman of industry association Dutch Cloud Community, the risks are substantial. “If your data are in a data centre of a cloud provider with a US UBO, then the US government can access them. It does not make a damn bit of difference if the data centre is in Europe.”

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0008
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0009
text: The industry association warns that technological dependence can put countries and companies in a tight spot, especially if geopolitical relations worsen. For example, Amsterdam Trade Bank (ATB), a subsidiary of Russia’s Alfa Bank, was cut off from IT services by Amazon and Microsoft after the invasion of Ukraine, causing major disruption: employees lost access to applications and email, and digital payments ground to a halt. It fuels fears of a scenario in which US tech companies stop providing services to Europe if political pressure mounts. “If the United States want to put pressure on us, they can do it via IT,” says Besteman.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0009
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0010
text: US clouds have grown steadily in recent years, even faster than local providers. Synergy Research calculated that between 2017 and 2022 the European cloud market became five times larger, but the combined [market share](https://www.srgresearch.com/articles/european-cloud-providers-continue-to-grow-but-still-lose-market-share#:~:text=New%20data%20from%20Synergy%20Research,and%20Deutsche%20Telekom%20are%20the) of European providers fell from 27 per cent to 13 per cent. Amazon, Microsoft and Google invest billions every quarter in new European data centres.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0010
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0011
text: European providers are therefore retreating to niches: they serve specific local needs or partner with the big three in hybrid arrangements. For example, French firm OVHcloud provides cloud services that meet strict European regulations. In some cases, organisations combine these services with those of Amazon Web Services or Microsoft Azure, so they can benefit from the scale and functionality of US platforms while keeping sensitive data within Europe and under European rules.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0011
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0012
text: The dominance of US cloud providers also extends to the backbone of artificial intelligence: the advanced servers on which AI models are trained and run. The United States accounts for about [70 per cent](https://geopolitique.eu/en/2025/02/10/financing-infrastructure-for-a-competitive-european-ai/#:~:text=A%20risk%20of%20strategic%20dependence,higher%20than%20in%20the%20US.) of this global AI compute capacity, the vast majority — 80 per cent — of which is in the hands of the hyperscalers. Europe has to make do with just 4 per cent of global capacity.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0012
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0013
text: This uneven distribution is indicative of Europe’s position in the broader AI race. European AI startups raise far less capital than their US counterparts. In 2024, around 90 billion dollars went to US AI startups and scale-ups, according to Dealroom data. In Europe, that amount stalled at just under 16 billion. According to Maarten Cleeren of Techleap, that is problematic. “In ten years’ time there will be no company that does not use AI. If we do not put money into the tech companies that support this, we have a problem.”

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0013
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0014
text: For now, European AI startups are fishing in a much smaller financing pond and are less likely to grow into heavyweights. The investments that such AI heavyweights make, in turn, are enormous. US company OpenAI, known for the smart chatbot ChatGPT, teamed up earlier this year with compatriot Oracle and Japan’s SoftBank under the name Stargate. In this joint venture, the technology companies will invest at least 500 billion dollars in AI infrastructure over the coming years.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0014
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0015
text: The fact that China’s share of global AI investment is relatively low is mainly because innovation there is largely driven by large technology companies such as Baidu, Tencent and Alibaba, which work closely with the government. Due to limited transparency in the capital market, many investments also remain out of sight.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0015
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0016
text: The results of China’s efforts are, however, attracting increasing attention. For example, Chinese startup Butterfly Effect recently launched the AI agent [Manus](https://www.businessinsider.com/manus-ai-china-agent-hype-deepseek-2025-3?international=true&r=US&IR=T#:~:text=,others%20are%20calling%20it%20overhyped), which performs complex tasks from start to finish with minimal human input. The AI model DeepSeek caused amazement at the end of 2024 because it was developed with far less compute power, yet achieved results comparable to the well-known language models of, among others, OpenAI and Meta. This makes the AI race not only a contest between US tech giants, but rather a global race with the United States and China at its centre. Europe has so far failed to secure a serious position in this race.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0016
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0017
text: The AI Act adopted by the European Commission in March 2024 is the world’s first comprehensive legislative package for artificial intelligence. The regulation imposes strict requirements on so-called high-risk applications and faces resistance in parts of the tech sector. “Potentially disastrous,” said industry association EU Tech Chamber of the AI Act; the rules would impose “stringent obligations” that [stifle](https://www.euronews.com/next/2023/12/15/potentially-disastrous-for-innovation-tech-sector-says-eu-ai-act-goes-too-far#:~:text=%27Potentially%20disastrous%27%3A%20Tech%20sector%20says,that%20underpin%20many%20downstream) innovation. Some AI entrepreneurs fear high compliance costs and legal uncertainty. “Founders do not know exactly where the boundaries lie,” says Cleeren of Techleap. “Companies will be reluctant to bring certain AI features to the European market that are launched in the United States.”

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0017
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0018
text: That uncertainty does not stand alone. Europe is known for its strong regulation, but for fast-growing technology companies it rarely works to their advantage. Startups that want to scale up face 27 national tax systems, labour laws and insolvency rules. Each country has its own interpretation of corporate tax, shareholder structures and employment protection — something that hinders rapid international growth. According to the [State of European Tech report](https://www.investeurope.eu/media/g3yobbcs/soet2024_report.pdf), 47 per cent of founders and investors see regulation and policymaking as a major barrier to growth.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0018
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0019
text: Another frequently cited frustration is limited access to growth capital. Since 2015, European startups in the growth phase have raised around 300 billion dollars, but according to the authors of the report this could have been double if they could as easily close large rounds as in the US. European companies make it through the first funding rounds, but fall back at investments from 15 million dollars: only 4.1 per cent reach that threshold, compared to 8.3 per cent in the US. One reason is the lack of deep capital buffers. European pension funds, for example, invest only 0.01 per cent of their assets in global venture capital, more than three times less than their US counterparts. As a result, it remains difficult for European startups to scale up to the kind of tech companies that underpin digital autonomy.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0019
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0020
text: ## Europe relies heavily on foreign chip production

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0020
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0021
text: But without physical industry, capital is not enough. This is painfully clear in the chip sector, where Europe relies heavily on foreign production capacity. Only 6 per cent of global chip production takes place in Europe and the Middle East. Asia accounts for more than 80 per cent of production. The United States supplies 8 per cent of all chips, but has the greatest control over design, patents and software. Europe lacks that control. Most chips made here are relatively simple: for cars, household appliances and industrial applications. Advanced chips — needed for smartphones, AI or military technology — mainly come from Taiwan and South Korea.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0021
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0022
text: ASML is Europe’s trump card. The Veldhoven-based company is the only one in the world that supplies machines for “extreme ultraviolet lithography” (EUV), the technology used to make the most advanced chips. But ASML supplies the world, not just Europe. Germany has strong positions in power electronics with Infineon and Bosch; chips that switch and control electrical energy efficiently, for example in cars and solar panels. Players such as the Dutch NXP and the Franco-Swiss STMicroelectronics focus on “embedded chips”. These are microchips built into devices or vehicles to perform a single specific task, such as controlling a motor or reading a sensor. For the most advanced chips, Europe has no factories and depends mainly on Taiwan and South Korea.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0022
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0023
text: The 2024 Draghi report identifies the lack of production capacity as a strategic risk. Taiwan, home to the world’s largest chipmaker TSMC, is considered by China to be a renegade province and has lived for years under the threat of military annexation. In a crisis, the supply of chips can stop abruptly. Even in segments where Europe is traditionally strong, such as automotive chips, vulnerability is high. During the coronavirus pandemic, car factories came to a standstill due to a shortage of simple chips. Large European chip companies — such as NXP, Infineon and STMicroelectronics — design these, but have them largely produced at foundries in Asia. There, car brands are relatively small customers, which meant they were lower on the priority list when demand returned after the first lockdowns.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0023
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0024
text: There is movement, however. On multiple fronts, there is a growing awareness in Europe that technological autonomy is only achievable with substantial investment, smart regulation and local alternatives. “Within the Dutch government, the question is no longer whether we should reduce dependence on US cloud companies, but how,” says Besteman of the Dutch Cloud Community. The House of Representatives recently adopted a motion calling on the government to tender for a “state cloud under full Dutch control”.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0024
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0025
text: Besteman does not expect such a government cloud to appear overnight, but such an intention can set the local cloud market in motion. “If you want European cloud providers to invest further in their services, you first need a buying signal.” Similar discussions are now taking place in the business community, Besteman notes. “They are also considering how safe their data really are, and what a plan B could be — an exit strategy.”

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0025
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0026
text: Europe is not aiming for a single alternative cloud solution that can match Microsoft Azure or Amazon Web Services in scale, but for a network of providers between which data and workloads can be moved and distributed easily. ECOFED is an example of such a “federated cloud”. The Dutch project is developing a solution that decouples software from the physical cloud infrastructure, giving users more freedom in choosing and combining cloud services.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0026
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0027
text: The Dutch company LeaseWeb is also making its voice heard. In March, the cloud provider presented the first results of its contribution to the IPCEI-CIS project — the same initiative that includes ECOFED. The company is building a cloud platform specifically designed to comply with European laws and regulations. The platform forms the heart of LeaseWeb’s so-called European Cloud Campus — a broader investment in sovereign cloud technology, local data centres and collaboration with knowledge institutions.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0027
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0028
text: Europe is also trying to gain ground in artificial intelligence. In February 2025, the European Commission launched the InvestAI initiative, intended to unlock 200 billion euros in investment in artificial intelligence. On 10 April, the AI Continent Plan was presented, which includes thirteen “AI factories” and even five “giga factories”. These facilities will make high-quality infrastructure available for training large models.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0028
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0029
text: Technology companies are also taking plenty of initiative. The Iliad Group, a French tech and telecoms conglomerate, announced in February an investment of 3 billion euros in sovereign AI infrastructure. It is investing in the large data centres of subsidiary OpCore and serves the market with AI compute via cloud company Scaleway. Mistral, a French company that develops advanced AI models itself, is one of Scaleway’s customers. France wants to become an important hub for artificial intelligence.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0029
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0030
text: An analysis by consultant McKinsey shows that Europe has secured an important place within [different links](https://www.mckinsey.com/~/media/mckinsey/business%20functions/quantumblack/our%20insights/time%20to%20place%20our%20bets%20europes%20ai%20opportunity/time-to-place-our-bets-europes-ai-opportunity.pdf) of the AI value chain. This applies, for example, to the machines used to make advanced chips, in which Eindhoven-based ASML has a virtual monopoly. IT service providers, including Capgemini, Ordina and Tietoevry, which help companies embed AI in their processes, also have a relatively large market share. Although Europe lags in the development of so-called foundation models — the AI models trained on vast amounts of data and able to generate new content — the consultant says Europe’s market share is growing. The same applies to the applications developed on the basis of these models.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0030
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0031
text: In the Netherlands, Cleeren of Techleap also sees AI companies excelling in specific areas and thus securing an important position within the value chain. “Weaviate uses AI to find and understand information based on what you mean, not just what you type,” he says. “And Deeploy is a platform that helps companies with a responsible implementation of AI.”

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0031
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0032
text: The narrative that Europe is unfriendly to tech entrepreneurs is giving way to a new story. In the State of European Tech 2024, a large majority of European founders said they would incorporate their company in Europe again if they could make that choice anew. The current geopolitical situation can even act as a flywheel, according to Cleeren: “America is turning inward, the market is destabilising. This creates space for companies in Europe.”

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0032
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0033
text: The European Commission, meanwhile, wants to put an end to the patchwork of laws and regulations with the “28th regime”. This allows a company to register under a set of rules that apply in all member states. The plans are part of the broader European Innovation Act. It should make it easier for startups to raise growth capital, including across borders. At present, investors run into different tax rules, supervisors and legal structures in each country. Europe wants to make that simpler, so that funds and investors can operate more smoothly in multiple countries.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0033
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0034
text: Reforms should also make it more attractive for startups to reward staff with share options. Since 2019, eleven countries have relaxed their national regulations, including Germany, France, Spain and the Netherlands. The European Commission is now investigating whether an overarching system is possible, as part of broader efforts to improve the business climate.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0034
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0035
text: At the same time, there is growing political pressure to get pension funds to play a more active role. They currently put an average of only 0.007 per cent of their assets into venture capital. In theory they can invest larger amounts, but strict rules and internal guidelines discourage this. European institutions such as the Commission, the Investment Bank and the Investment Fund see pension funds as a key link in increasing the amount of growth capital in Europe, and are exploring ways to stimulate this — for example through risk-sharing via EU funds or adjusted capital requirements.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0035
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0036
text: With the European Chips Act, Europe took a first step in 2023 to reduce its dependence on semiconductors. With 43 billion euros in public and private investment, Europe wants to double its own market share in chip production to 20 per cent by 2030. On the one hand, Europe is trying to catch up in advanced chips; on the other, it wants to build further on its existing strength in simpler chips.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0036
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0037
text: There are plenty of initiatives in the latter category. Local champion STMicroelectronics is expanding production, including by building a new factory in Italy. A factory by Taiwan’s TSMC is coming to Dresden in cooperation with European players Bosch, Infineon and NXP. Production of advanced chips, by contrast, has yet to get off the ground. The planned megafactory of GlobalFoundries and STMicroelectronics in France has been put on hold, and construction of Intel’s advanced factory in Germany has been pushed back to 2029.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0037
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0038
text: Because the Chips Act has ultimately not delivered enough, the European Commission is considering a “Chips Act 2.0”. The chip industry sees merit in a programme that supports the entire chain, from design and materials to machines and factories. From the European Parliament come calls for investment in AI chips, but the industry itself argues for continued support for older chip technology too, so that chips for cars, machines and power grids can also come from Europe. The new programme will be presented before the summer.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0038
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0039
text: To seize all the opportunities, structural measures and substantial investment are necessary. According to the Draghi report, 800 billion euros in public-private spending is needed annually to make Europe more technologically independent and economically resilient. The investments must focus on high-tech sectors such as semiconductors and artificial intelligence, but also on decarbonisation and defence.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0039
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0040
text: Europe has strong assets to build on: renowned knowledge institutions, a large internal market and niche champions such as ASML and Mistral. At the same time, the gap with the US and China is considerable, especially in cloud infrastructure, advanced chips and AI. The coming years will show whether Europe can narrow that gap.

metadata.chunk_id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0040
metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0041
text: For years, Europe was mainly a regulator: influential, but reactive. Now the realisation has sunk in that regulation alone is not enough and that Europe itself must invest in technology, infrastructure and production capacity. The contours of digital autonomy are taking shape, although Europe will have to act faster and sharper than before.

metadata.source_slug: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid

id: Europa zet in op technologische zelfredzaamheid__a2-late-chunking__0042
text: Technology, Media & Telecom (TMT) is one of the fastest-growing sectors. Helped by the increasing use of software, algorithms and sensors, this sector underpins the digital renewal of other sectors.
